no_solution_leakage.reason: The instruction essentially gives the complete solution: exact function structure (install_rustup), exact variable names (rustup_sha256s, rustup_targets), exact environment variables with exact values, exact curl flags, exact sha256sum command, exact architecture detection method, exact cleanup steps, exact timeout values, exact PATH changes. tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: The f2p tests (test_install_rustup_function_in_script, test_dockerfile_has_rustup_install, etc.) are almost entirely 'assert X in open(path).read()' string-matching tests. The pass-to-pass tests do run shellcheck, bash -n, yamllint, and pytest as subprocesses which verify behavior, but the core f2p tests only check text content of files, never executing the install_rustup function or building any Docker image.

test_not_tautological.reason: The f2p tests check for specific strings (SHA256 hashes, function definitions, variable names) that would not exist in the base commit files. A stub/empty implementation would not pass since the base files don't contain 'install_rustup', 'rustup_sha256s', etc.

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution exists at /workspace/airflow accessible in the container (the Dockerfile clones the repo and checks out a specific commit). The agent could read solution/solve.sh if it exists in the test environment, or could simply look at the gold patch commit (1b2893300d8b6c3ae656b8e7d9a44138b663d633) which is fetched in the Dockerfile. The agent could run 'git diff c4a209ba..1b289330' to see the exact solution.

behavior_in_tests.reason: All behaviors described in instruction.md are tested: SHA256 verification, architecture handling, curl security flags, environment variables in both Dockerfiles, PATH cleanup in Dockerfile.ci, timeout updates in CI workflow, and the install_rustup function definition.

structured_data_schema.reason: This task produces shell scripts and Dockerfiles, not structured data like JSON/CSV/API/DB.
